methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series D → next-stage step-up multiplier (1.5x) to $174M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.skip_reason: insufficient narrow peer set in Drones & Autonomous (found 0 same-stage, 0 adjacent-stage, 0 similar-tier; need ≥3)
methods.rationale: 5 same-sector peers exist in Drones & Autonomous, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

private.name: Oxa
private.rationale: Same sector (Drones & Autonomous), same stage (Series D)

data.narrative:
Zeitview, a Series D autonomous systems company founded by Dan Burton, is evaluated within the Drones & Autonomous sector. The broader peer universe comprises six private companies including Zipline, Joby Aviation, Nuro, Archer Aviation, and Elroy Air; no public comparables exist in this nascent sector.

Valuation analysis relies on the Stage-Progression method, as a narrow peer set at identical stage and valuation tier proved insufficient for meaningful Peer-Median triangulation. The company has raised $174M to date. Applying a 1.5x step-up multiplier typical of Series D progression yields an implied valuation of $261M. This methodology assumes Zeitview's capital efficiency and market position align with historical stage-transition patterns in frontier autonomous systems.

The triangulated range supports valuations between $222M (low) and $300M (high), with $261M as the median point. Material uncertainties remain: the absence of mature public comparables limits benchmarking precision; the fragmented competitive landscape in autonomous systems creates wide dispersion in unit economics and timeline-to-profitability across peers; and Series D funding dynamics in this sector remain volatile. Any valuation within the stated range should be stress-tested against Zeitview's specific burn rate, capital efficiency, and pathway to Series E or growth funding relative to sector peers.
